metadata.excerpt: Virtual servers can use default persistence profiles to ensure that subsequent client connections bypass load balancing and consistently return to the same pool member. ... Virtual servers can also...

content.sfdetails__c:
Description

Virtual servers can use default persistence profiles to ensure that subsequent client connections bypass load balancing and consistently return to the same pool member. Virtual servers can also use a Fallback persistence profile to create a secondary or fallback persistence record for each new client connection. The system evaluates subsequent client connections using persistence data provided by the client and locally stored persistence records. This overview describes in further detail the logic behind Fallback persistence as well as recommendations for when you should use it.

The virtual server's Default Persistence Profile option allows the system to use many different persistence profiles. For example; Cookie, Secure Sockets Layer (SSL), Session Initial Protocol (SIP), etc. The virtual server's Fallback Persistence Profile option allows the system to use only two persistence profiles:

  • Destination Address Affinity (dest_addr) - Directs session requests to the same server based solely on the destination IP address of a packet.
  • Source Address Affinity (source_addr) - Directs session requests to the same server based solely on the source IP address of a packet.

When only the Default Persistence Profile is selected, processing client connections is fairly simple; if a new client connection matches a persistence record, the timeout value for the persistence record is updated and the client is sent to the associated pool member. However, when both Default and Fallback Persistence Profiles are selected, understanding how client connections are processed becomes slightly more complicated and the system may behave differently than expected. The virtual server applies the following logic when both persistence profile types are selected and a new connection is received:

  1. Determine the persistence mode based on the associated persistence profiles.
  2. Look for a matching Default persist record.
    • If a Default persistence record is found, use this record to direct the client connection.
  3. If there is no Default persistence record, look for a matching Fallback persist record.
    • If a Fallback persistence record is found, use this record to direct the client connection.
  4. If no Primary or Fallback persistence records are found, use the configured load balancing algorithm to direct the client connection.
  5. After the LB decision is made (see above):
    • Update both Default and Fallback persistence records with an idle time of 0 and destination of the selected pool member.

Environment

  • Fallback Persistence

Cause

None

Recommended Actions

Enabling the Fallback Persistence Profile

You should consider Fallback persistence helpful in the following situations:

  • The remote client has no means of providing, or is blocking, persistence data. For example, the client is blocking or is unable to use Cookies.
  • The client-provided persistence data may change and cause a load balancing decision that results in the system pointing to a new pool member. For example, the SSL Session-ID changes.

Viewing Persistence Records

You can use the following command syntax to view the persistence record entries for a specific client, and the information each record contains, such as the idle timeout period:

tmsh show ltm persistence persist-records client-addr <IP_Addr> all-properties

For example, to view the persistence record entries for a specific client using IP address 10.12.23.27, use the following command:

tmsh show ltm persistence persist-records client-addr 10.12.23.27 all-properties
Sys::Persistent Connections

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TMM 0

Age (sec.) 8

Virtual Addr 10.12.23.205:443
Node Addr 10.12.23.27:80
Pool Name /Common/example_pool
Client Addr 10.12.23.27

Additional Information

None
